blogs.title: Web Development in 2025: A Year in Review
blogs.description: A look back at the major trends, technologies, and paradigm shifts that shaped the world of web development in 2025.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
A Transformative Year

2025 was a year of rapid evolution in web development, driven by advancements in AI, a focus on performance, and the maturation of existing frameworks. As we look back, several key themes stand out.

1. AI Becomes a Mainstream Co-Pilot

If 2024 was the year of AI hype, 2025 was the year of practical integration. Tools like GitHub Copilot became indispensable, and AI-powered agents capable of writing, debugging, and even deploying code moved from experiment to production. The role of the developer shifted towards becoming an 'AI orchestrator', defining goals and validating the output of their AI partners.

2. The Edge Becomes Standard

Edge computing, once a niche for performance enthusiasts, became a standard part of the deployment pipeline. Frameworks like Next.js and SvelteKit made deploying functions to the edge trivial. Developers embraced running logic closer to users to deliver personalization, A/B testing, and authentication with near-zero latency.

3. The Rise of 'Local-First' Architecture

Frustration with slow, network-dependent applications led to a surge in interest in local-first development. Using technologies like CRDTs and tools like ElectricSQL, developers began building applications that work seamlessly offline and sync in the background, providing a vastly superior and more resilient user experience.

4. Component-Driven CSS

Modern CSS features like Container Queries and the `:has()` selector finally hit mainstream adoption. This enabled the creation of truly modular components that adapt to their container, not the viewport, fulfilling a long-held dream of component-driven design.

2025 was a year where we built smarter, faster, and more resilient applications, setting the stage for even more exciting innovations to come.

blogs.title: Crystal Ball: My Top 5 Tech Predictions for 2026
blogs.description: From ambient computing to the AI-driven refactoring of legacy systems, here are my predictions for the technologies that will define 2026.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
What's Next on the Horizon?

As we close out 2025, it's time to look forward. Technology moves at a blistering pace, but certain trends give us a glimpse of the future. Here are my top five predictions for 2026.

1. AI Will Tackle Legacy Codebases

Specialized AI agents will be trained to understand and refactor massive, decades-old legacy codebases (think COBOL and old Java systems). This will unlock huge business value and be a major new market for AI services.

2. Spatial Computing Finds Its Killer App

While devices like the Vision Pro have been impressive, 2026 will be the year that spatial computing finds its first true 'killer app' outside of niche industrial uses. I predict it will be in the realm of collaborative design or data visualization.

3. The Great 'SaaS-ification' of Everything

More and more complex software, from creative tools to development environments, will move entirely to the browser, delivered as a SaaS product. Advancements in WebAssembly and streaming technology will make this possible.

4. Biometric Authentication Becomes the Default

Passkeys will see mass adoption, and logging in with a password will start to feel archaic and insecure. Biometric-based logins will become the default for new applications, significantly improving security and user experience.

5. Personalized, Generative UI

We will see the first examples of interfaces that are generated and personalized in real-time by AI based on a user's context, goals, and past behavior. Instead of a one-size-fits-all UI, every user will have an experience tailored specifically to them.

blogs.title: How to Contribute to Open Source: A Beginner's Guide
blogs.description: Ready to give back? This guide breaks down the steps to making your first contribution to an open source project, from finding a project to submitting your first pull request.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
Why Contribute to Open Source?

Contributing to open source is one of the best ways to improve your skills, build your resume, and give back to the community that provides the tools we use every day. It can be intimidating at first, but the process is more straightforward than you might think.

Step 1: Find a Project

The best project to contribute to is one you already use and love. You're already familiar with it, which makes it easier to find areas for improvement. If you're looking for something new, websites like CodeTriage and GitHub's own explore page are great places to start. Look for projects with a `good first issue` or `help wanted` label.

Step 2: Understand the Project and the Contribution Process

Before you write any code, take the time to read the project's `README.md` and `CONTRIBUTING.md` files. These will explain the project's setup, coding standards, and the process for submitting changes. Set up the project locally and make sure you can run its tests.

Step 3: Pick an Issue and Claim It

Find a small bug or a documentation typo to start with. Leave a comment on the issue letting the maintainers know you'd like to work on it. This prevents multiple people from working on the same thing.

Step 4: Make Your Changes and Submit a Pull Request (PR)

Create a new branch for your changes. Once you've fixed the issue, push your branch to your fork and open a Pull Request to the main project's repository. Your PR description should clearly explain what you changed and why, and it should reference the issue number it resolves (e.g., 'Fixes #123').

# Example Git workflow

git checkout -b my-bug-fix

# ... make your code changes ...

git commit -m "fix: Correct typo in documentation (fixes #123)"

git push origin my-bug-fix

# ... then open a PR on GitHub ...

Your first PR might require some changes based on feedback from the maintainers. Don't be discouraged! This is part of the collaborative process. Congratulations, you're now an open source contributor!

blogs.title: My Favorite Developer Productivity Tools for 2026
blogs.description: A curated list of the tools and software that save me time and make me a more effective developer, from terminal emulators to AI-powered assistants.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
Sharpening the Saw

As developers, our tools are critical to our effectiveness. The right set of tools can save hours of time and reduce cognitive load. Here are the tools that I find indispensable in my daily workflow heading into 2026.

1. Warp Terminal

Warp is a modern, Rust-based terminal that reimagines the command-line experience. It groups commands and their output into blocks, has an integrated AI assistant, and provides an experience that feels more like a modern code editor than a traditional terminal.

2. VS Code with a Curated Extension Set

VS Code is still my editor of choice. My must-have extensions are:

  • GitHub Copilot: An indispensable AI pair programmer.
  • Error Lens: Inline error and warning messages, so I don't have to hover.
  • GitLens: Supercharges the built-in Git capabilities, making it easy to see who changed a line of code and why.

3. Raycast (macOS)

Raycast is a supercharged replacement for Spotlight. It's a command palette for your entire OS. I use it to launch apps, search files, manage my clipboard history, run scripts, and so much more. It's extensible and has a thriving community building new features for it.

4. Postman / Insomnia

For API development and testing, Postman and Insomnia are essential. They provide a powerful UI for making HTTP requests, organizing them into collections, and writing automated API tests.

// Example of an API test in Postman
pm.test("Status code is 200", function () {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});
pm.test("Response has correct user ID", function () {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(jsonData.id).to.eql(1);
});

Investing time in mastering your tools has one of the highest ROIs for any developer. What are your favorite tools?

blogs.title: The Rise of Platform Engineering: Empowering Developers
blogs.description: What is Platform Engineering and why is it replacing traditional DevOps? Learn how building internal developer platforms (IDPs) can accelerate your entire organization.
blogs.author: Sunil Khobragade
blogs.content:
The Evolution of DevOps

For years, DevOps has been about breaking down the silos between development and operations. However, in many organizations, this has led to developers being overwhelmed with operational complexity. They now have to manage CI/CD pipelines, Kubernetes manifests, and cloud infrastructure, taking time away from writing application code.

Enter Platform Engineering

Platform Engineering is the discipline of designing and building an Internal Developer Platform (IDP). An IDP is a set of tools and processes, curated by the platform team, that provides a paved road for developers to build and deploy their applications. The goal is to provide a self-service experience that abstracts away the underlying infrastructure complexity.

What's in an IDP?

An IDP is composed of many different tools, unified into a cohesive platform. This might include:

  • CI/CD Pipelines: Standardized, reusable workflows for building and testing.
  • Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Modules: Pre-configured Terraform or CloudFormation modules for provisioning resources.
  • Service Templates: Pre-built starter kits for creating new applications with best practices baked in.
  • A Developer Portal: A central UI (like Backstage.io) where developers can see their services, access documentation, and provision new resources.

By treating their platform as a product, platform engineering teams can significantly improve the developer experience, increase productivity, and ensure that all applications are built in a secure and compliant way. It's about enabling developers, not burdening them.
